title: Read and Write User Input in Java
body:
`Scanner` is the standard way to read user input from the console in Java. It wraps `System.in` and parses tokens.

```java
Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
String name = sc.nextLine();
int age = sc.nextInt();
```

Key `Scanner` methods:
- `next()` — next whitespace-delimited token
- `nextLine()` — entire line
- `nextInt()`, `nextDouble()`, `nextBoolean()` — typed input
- `hasNext()`, `hasNextInt()` — check before reading

**Common pitfall:** calling `nextInt()` followed by `nextLine()` leaves a newline in the buffer. Call `sc.nextLine()` to consume it before the next `nextLine()`.

For output, use `System.out.print()` (no newline), `System.out.println()` (with newline), or `System.out.printf()` for formatted output. Always close `Scanner` when done.

examples.label: Reading input with Scanner

title: Java – Create a File
body:
Java NIO2 (java.nio.file) provides the recommended way to create files.

**`Files.createFile(path)`** — creates a new empty file. Throws `FileAlreadyExistsException` if it exists.

**`Files.createDirectory(path)`** — creates a directory.

**`Files.createDirectories(path)`** — creates directory and all missing parents.

**`Files.createTempFile(prefix, suffix)`** — creates a temporary file in the system temp directory.

Legacy approach with `java.io.File`:
```java
new File("output.txt").createNewFile(); // returns false if already exists
```

The NIO2 approach is preferred because it throws descriptive exceptions rather than returning booleans. Always handle `IOException`. Check if a file exists first with `Files.exists(path)` to avoid exceptions.

examples.label: Creating files and directories with NIO2

title: Java – Write to File

examples.label: Writing with Files.writeString and append

title: Introduction to the Java NIO2 File API
body:
**NIO2** (New I/O 2), introduced in Java 7 as `java.nio.file`, provides a modern, comprehensive file system API.

Core classes:
- **`Path`** — represents a file system path (immutable)
- **`Paths`** / `Path.of()` — creates `Path` objects
- **`Files`** — static utility methods for file operations
- **`FileSystem`** / `FileSystems` — access different file systems
- **`WatchService`** — watch directory for changes

Key `Files` operations:
- `copy(src, dst)`, `move(src, dst)`, `delete(path)`
- `readAllBytes()`, `readAllLines()`, `readString()`
- `write()`, `writeString()`
- `walk(path)` — recursive directory traversal
- `find(start, depth, matcher)` — search with BiPredicate
- `getAttribute()`, `getLastModifiedTime()`

NIO2 handles symbolic links, file attributes, and cross-platform path differences correctly.

examples.label: Directory walk with Files.walk()

title: Guide to Java OutputStream
body:
`OutputStream` is the abstract base for all byte-based output streams. It is the counterpart to `InputStream` for writing binary data.

**Core methods:**
- `write(int b)` — write a single byte
- `write(byte[] bytes)` — write all bytes
- `write(byte[] bytes, offset, len)` — write a range
- `flush()` — force buffered data to the underlying stream
- `close()` — close and release resources

**Common implementations:**
- `FileOutputStream` — write to a file
- `ByteArrayOutputStream` — write to an in-memory byte array
- `BufferedOutputStream` — buffered wrapper for performance
- `DataOutputStream` — write primitive types as bytes
- `ObjectOutputStream` — serialize objects

Always wrap with `BufferedOutputStream` for file I/O to reduce system calls. Use `OutputStreamWriter` to bridge from character streams to byte streams with charset control.

examples.label: Writing bytes to a file

title: Formatting Output with printf() in Java
body:
`System.out.printf()` (and `String.format()`) use printf-style format strings to produce formatted output.

**Format specifiers:**
- `%d` — integer
- `%f` — floating point (default 6 decimal places)
- `%.2f` — 2 decimal places
- `%s` — string
- `%n` — platform-appropriate newline
- `%5d` — width 5, right-aligned
- `%-5d` — width 5, left-aligned
- `%05d` — zero-padded
- `%e` — scientific notation
- `%x` — hexadecimal
- `%b` — boolean
- `%c` — character

`String.format()` returns the formatted string without printing. `%n` is preferred over `\n` for portability. Java 15+ `formatted()` is an instance method on String: `"Value: %d".formatted(42)`.

examples.label: Formatted output
